prompt: We need to replace our HTTP client library across 200 call sites in a live production service. We can't take a feature freeze to do it. What's the approach?
expected: Applies Branch By Abstraction: introduce a thin HTTPClient interface wrapping the current library, migrate all 200 call sites to the interface incrementally, then implement the new library behind the interface and remove the old one. The code is never broken and the migration spans normal work over weeks with no feature freeze or git migration branch.
notes: must include: abstraction, interface, incrementally, never broken; must not include: feature freeze, git branch; source id: tp01

prompt: We're using a deprecated logging library that's imported in 80 packages. We want to switch to slog but can't do it in one PR. How do we manage this migration?
expected: Applies Branch By Abstraction: define a Logger interface shaped around how callers use logging, implement it with the current library, incrementally migrate packages to the interface, then swap the implementation to slog and remove the old library.
notes: must include: interface, incremental, abstraction; source id: tp02

prompt: We want to migrate from Gin to a newer web framework. We have 60 route handlers. We can't stop feature work for a month to do a big-bang migration. What's the strategy?
expected: Applies Branch By Abstraction: define a handler interface decoupled from Gin's types, back it with a Gin adapter, migrate the 60 handlers through the interface one by one, then replace the adapter with the new framework. Rejects a big-bang cutover; feature work continues throughout.
notes: must include: interface, incremental, abstraction; must not include: big-bang; source id: tp03

prompt: We have a messy subsystem that needs to be rewritten. Management won't give us a refactoring sprint. How do we make progress on it without stopping feature work?
expected: Advises gradually working on the subsystem over weeks — whenever anyone touches code in the refactoring zone, move it a little toward the target state — and, for replacing a component, introduce an abstraction interface first so callers migrate incrementally without ever breaking the build. No dedicated sprint is needed.
notes: must include: gradually, incremental, abstraction; source id: tp04

prompt: We need to extract a section of code into a shared library that two teams can use. It's currently embedded in service A and called from 30 places. How do we do this without breaking service A during the extraction?
expected: Applies Branch By Abstraction: introduce an interface representing the functionality, implement it in place using the embedded code, migrate all 30 call sites to the interface, then move the implementation to the new shared library location. Service A never breaks during the extraction.
notes: must include: interface, migrate callers, never breaks; source id: tp05

prompt: We want to replace our gRPC client wrapper library. Multiple team members are working on different features simultaneously. If we do the migration, how do we avoid conflicts and keep the build green for everyone?
expected: Applies Branch By Abstraction: land the interface abstraction first in a single PR, after which any member can migrate call sites incrementally in their own PRs — each file compiles whether it uses the old direct call or the new interface, so the build stays green. The new implementation is wired in only after all call sites are on the interface.
notes: must include: interface first, incremental, build stays green; source id: tp06

prompt: I want to create a git branch for my refactoring work so I don't disrupt the main branch while I clean things up. What should I name it?
expected: Skill should not activate: this is a git branch-naming question, not Branch By Abstraction (which is a code-level migration strategy and is explicitly an alternative to long-lived refactoring branches). Answer the git naming question directly.
notes: must not include: Branch By Abstraction as the primary recommendation; source id: tp07

prompt: I want to rename the function `processData` to `transformRecords` across the codebase. It's called in about 15 places. How do I do this safely?
expected: Skill should not activate: this is a simple rename that fits a single IDE refactoring or single commit (Change Function Declaration), not a weeks-long component replacement. No abstraction interface or migration plan is needed.
notes: must include: rename, single commit; must not include: Branch By Abstraction, introduce an interface; source id: tp08

prompt: We're deprecating v1 of our public REST API and want to migrate our customers to v2. How do we manage the transition?
expected: Skill should not activate: the callers are external consumers the team does not control, so call sites cannot be migrated. The right technique is API versioning with a deprecation window (run v1 and v2 in parallel, communicate the timeline, sunset v1), not Branch By Abstraction.
notes: must include: API versioning, deprecation; must not include: Branch By Abstraction as the primary technique; source id: tp09

prompt: We have a database ORM we want to replace and it's used everywhere — both the ORM calls (which are in application code) and the schema (which needs to change as part of the migration to the new ORM's conventions). How do we approach this?
expected: Both skills apply and are complementary: Branch By Abstraction handles the code side (introduce a repository interface, back it with the current ORM, migrate callers, then implement with the new ORM), while the schema change — add columns, support old+new simultaneously, migrate data, drop old columns — requires fowler-database-parallel-change (expand-contract).
notes: must include: repository interface, incremental, schema; source id: tp10

prompt: We want to replace our internal payment processing service with a third-party provider. Our application makes gRPC calls to the internal service from about 40 places. How do we migrate?
expected: Branch By Abstraction applies to the code side — define a PaymentProcessor interface, back it with the current gRPC client, migrate the 40 call sites, then implement with the third-party SDK — but the swap crosses a network boundary, so additional network-level concerns apply (traffic shadowing, rollback, monitoring the new provider before full cutover) beyond the standard in-process technique.
notes: must include: interface, incremental, network boundary; source id: tp11

prompt: We started migrating from our old HTTP client to a new one six months ago. We're about 60% through the call sites. The migration has stalled and now we have a mix of old and new code in the codebase. What should we do?
expected: Names this as the known partial-migration failure mode of Branch By Abstraction: either recommit — assign ownership and use CI/grep gates to track and eliminate remaining direct usages of the old library, completing the migration — or, if completion is impossible, standardize on one path. The worst outcome is living permanently in the split in-between state.
notes: must include: complete the migration, track remaining; source id: tp12
